personalNote:
I built the Value Clicks model at Action Network before I knew what content engineering meant as a category. The idea was simple: every user action on a piece of content carries some probability of converting to revenue. I modeled that by sport, content type, and reader geography, and it became the company's north star metric. Content strategy, product roadmap, and traffic investment all ran through that model afterward. AirOps is building the infrastructure to make that kind of deliberate content production accessible to every team, not just the ones that have someone like me writing SQL on the weekend.

What draws me to AirOps specifically is the motion. Build AI workflows internally, prove they work, then productize the best of them for customers. That is exactly what I did at Smarter Technologies: 22 skills, 57 integration scripts, a hooks-based feedback loop that routed usage signal directly back into skill iteration. By the time I ported the highest-impact tools to a shared co-work interface for non-technical teammates, the validation work was already done. I know what that loop feels like from the inside.

The four roles I evaluated cover a real range. The SA and FDE seats are the strongest fit on paper: the build-and-productize motion is documented at depth in my background, and I can start contributing immediately. The PM roles are an honest stretch, particularly PM Growth, where the PLG title history gap is structural. I included them because the underlying work has been real, and I thought you should see the full picture rather than a curated slice.

I am based in SF and would be in the office. AirOps raised its Series B from Greylock in November 2025. I paid attention to that. The thesis: content engineering as a category, AI-era search as the distribution surface, marketing and growth teams as the ICP. That is a coherent and well-timed bet, and I want to be part of building it.
